feature_name: 32 GPIO signals organized in two banks
description: The device provides up to 32 general-purpose I/O signals named GP[31:0]. These signals are organized into two banks of 16 pins each for control and status. Register fields map directly to individual GPIO pins within their respective banks. This organization simplifies per-pin configuration while enabling bank-level operations. Signal-to-bank mapping details are explicitly documented.
evidence: Section 2.2 Signal Descriptions; Section 2.5 GPIO Register Structure; Table 1
dependencies: Pin multiplexing configuration; Device package pinout
Remarks: Verify package pin availability and multiplexing before relying on specific GPIO indices.

feature_name: Per-pin direction control with default input
description: Each GPIO pin direction is software-configurable as input or output. The DIR01 register controls per-pin direction where 1 selects input and 0 selects output. All GPIO pins default to inputs after reset, ensuring safe power-up. Direction can be changed at runtime as needed. The bit positions correspond to the specific pin numbers within each bank.
dependencies: PSC clock enabled; Pin multiplexing completed
Remarks: Set direction before driving outputs to avoid unintended pin states.

feature_name: Atomic set/clear and direct output write
description: GPIO outputs can be controlled via three mechanisms. SET_DATA01 sets selected outputs high and CLR_DATA01 clears selected outputs low using write-1 semantics, enabling atomic multi-bit updates. OUT_DATA01 supports direct read-modify-write to assign full output patterns. Using separate set/clear registers allows multiple software processes to control outputs without critical-section protection. Reading SET/CLR/OUT registers returns register content, not necessarily the external pin level if configured as input.
evidence: Section 1.2 Features; Section 2.6; Sections 3.4 OUT_DATA01, 3.5 SET_DATA01, 3.6 CLR_DATA01
dependencies: DIR01 configured for outputs; PSC clock enabled
Remarks: Prefer SET/CLR writes for concurrency-safe updates; use OUT_DATA for full pattern updates when exclusive access is guaranteed.

feature_name: Synchronized input sampling and pin state readback
description: The IN_DATA01 register returns the current logic level for each GPIO pin. For inputs, the values are synchronized to the GPIO peripheral clock to avoid metastability. For outputs, IN_DATA01 returns the value being driven by the device, or the wired-logic level for open-drain cases. Reading OUT_DATA01 does not guarantee actual external pin levels, so IN_DATA01 should be used for verification. Open-drain behavior, if used, is device-specific and reflected at IN_DATA01 as wired-logic.
dependencies: PSC clock enabled; Potential open-drain external pull configuration (device-specific)
Remarks: Use IN_DATA01 to validate line state, especially for open-drain or shared-wire implementations.

feature_name: Per-pin edge-triggered interrupt generation
description: Each GPIO can generate an interrupt on rising edge, falling edge, both, or be disabled. Edge detection is synchronized to the GPIO peripheral clock. Configuration is done via SET_RIS_TRIG01/CLR_RIS_TRIG01 for rising edges and SET_FAL_TRIG01/CLR_FAL_TRIG01 for falling edges. Direction does not need to be input for interrupt generation; software-driven output toggles can intentionally trigger interrupts. This flexibility enables both event detection and loopback-style debug signaling.
dependencies: BINTEN bank enable; Interrupt controller routing (device-specific); PSC clock enabled
Remarks: Ensure only intended edges are enabled to avoid interrupt storms; debounce externally if needed.

feature_name: GPIO interrupt status and write-1-to-clear
description: Pending GPIO interrupt events are latched in INTSTAT01. A logic 1 in a bit position indicates a pending interrupt for the corresponding pin. Software clears pending status by writing a 1 to the same bit (write-1-to-clear). For individually routed GPIO interrupts, CPU flags may also reflect status, while bank interrupts use INTSTAT01 to identify the source. It is the software’s responsibility to service and clear all pending sources.
dependencies: Interrupt service routines; Proper clearing order
Remarks: Always clear the specific source in INTSTAT01 to prevent re-entry; read-modify-write is not required for W1C fields.

feature_name: Bank-level interrupt enable controlling interrupts and EDMA events
description: BINTEN enables or disables interrupt/event generation per 16-pin bank. Enabling EN0 and EN1 controls whether pins in bank 0 and bank 1 can generate CPU interrupts and EDMA events. This provides quick masking at the bank granularity. Reserved bits read as zero and should not be modified. Bank association for pins is fixed by pin index.
dependencies: Interrupt controller configuration; EDMA event mapping (device-specific)
Remarks: Use bank masking during bulk reconfiguration to avoid spurious events; restore enables after setup.

feature_name: EDMA synchronization via GPIO events
description: GPIO can generate synchronization events for the EDMA controller. These events can be used to trigger DMA transfers on external pin transitions. Event production follows the same edge configuration used for interrupts. Bank enable in BINTEN affects EDMA events as well. Specific EDMA event numbers and routing are device-manual defined.
dependencies: EDMA controller configuration; BINTEN enabled; Device-specific EDMA event IDs
Remarks: Use GPIO events for lightweight sync when dedicated peripheral triggers are unavailable; confirm latency requirements.

feature_name: Clock control and PSC enable with module clock at CPU/6
description: The GPIO peripheral clock is disabled by default and must be enabled via the local PSC. The GPIO input clock frequency is derived as CPU/6. Without enabling the PSC domain, register access and interrupt/event generation will not function. Clocking affects synchronization of input sampling and edge detection. Proper sequencing of clock enable is part of initialization.
dependencies: Power and Sleep Controller (PSC) configuration; CPU clock settings
Remarks: Enable PSC for GPIO before any configuration; consider clock gating for power savings when idle.

feature_name: Low-power behavior under PSC control
description: The PSC can place the GPIO module into reduced-power states. While in low-power, interrupt generation is suspended but output pin states are retained. When the module exits low-power, normal operation resumes with previously held outputs. Software must account for suspended event generation during sleep. Power management is centralized through the PSC for all peripherals.
dependencies: PSC low-power transitions; System power policy
Remarks: Quiesce interrupt sources before entering low power to avoid missed events; re-enable as needed after wake.

feature_name: Pin multiplexing control for GPIO function selection
description: The device uses extensive pin multiplexing to support many peripheral functions in limited package pins. GPIO function selection depends on device-specific mux settings at reset and software-configurable registers. Correct muxing is a prerequisite for GPIO operation on desired pins. Details are provided in the device data manual. Software must ensure that conflicting peripheral assignments are avoided.
dependencies: Device-specific pinmux registers; Boot-time mux state
Remarks: Apply pinmux before GPIO configuration; document mux ownership across subsystems to prevent contention.

feature_name: Initialization sequence after hardware reset
description: A defined sequence is required to bring GPIO online after hardware reset. First, configure device pin multiplexing for desired GPIO pins. Next, enable the GPIO module via PSC. Finally, program direction, data, and interrupt control registers for the target pins. After these steps, the GPIO module is ready for data transactions and event generation.
dependencies: Pinmux configured; PSC enabled; DIR/OUT/IN/INT registers programmed
Remarks: Follow the documented order to avoid writes to a clock-gated module; add sanity reads to confirm state.

feature_name: Reset behavior: software vs hardware vs PSC reset
description: Software resets via emulator do not modify GPIO configuration or pin states. A PSC reset sequence (clock disable, PSC reset, clock enable) restores default configuration register settings. A hardware reset resets configuration and data registers to defaults, affecting pin states. Understanding these distinctions is important for robust recovery paths. Default directions are inputs after full hardware or PSC reset.
dependencies: Reset controller behavior; Board-level reset distribution
Remarks: Design firmware to reinitialize GPIO after PSC or hardware reset; avoid assuming persistence across all reset types.

feature_name: Endianness-independent GPIO operation
description: GPIO operation is independent of CPU endianness mode. Register layouts and behavior are unaffected by endianness selection. This simplifies software portability across big- and little-endian builds. No additional byte-lane handling is required for GPIO accesses. The module requires no endianness-specific configuration.
dependencies: None
Remarks: Access registers using standard 32-bit reads/writes; endianness only affects memory data interpretation elsewhere.

feature_name: Emulation and debug considerations
description: GPIO is not affected by emulation suspend events such as halts and breakpoints. This means GPIO hardware continues operating while the CPU is halted. Additionally, when a pin is configured as an output, software can toggle it to intentionally generate an interrupt for connectivity testing. These characteristics aid non-intrusive observation and stimulus during debug. Careful coordination is needed to avoid unintended live interrupts while halted.
dependencies: Debugger behavior; Interrupt controller routing
Remarks: Mask bank interrupts when performing intrusive debug to prevent unexpected ISR entry on resume.

feature_name: Register structure and bit mapping per bank pair
description: GPIO control registers are organized as 32-bit registers covering a pair of banks (0 and 1). Field naming follows a consistent pattern mapping bit positions to GPIO indices. Table 1 details how each GPIO pin maps to register bits. This uniform structure applies across direction, data, edge-config, and status registers. The organization simplifies driver implementation and table-driven access.
evidence: Section 2.5 GPIO Register Structure; Table 1; Section 3 overview
dependencies: None
Remarks: Use common bit macros and masks across registers to minimize code duplication and errors.

feature_name: Open-drain signal considerations (device-specific)
description: Some signals may utilize open-drain output buffers for wired-logic operation. For such pins, reading IN_DATA01 returns the wired-logic level, which may not be driven solely by the device. External pull-up or pull-down components determine idle levels. Details of which pins support open-drain are found in the device data manual. Software should account for bus sharing and passive levels when using open-drain.
dependencies: External pull components; Device data manual; Pinmux
Remarks: Configure external resistors appropriately and verify bus timing; avoid enabling strong push-pull on shared lines.

feature_name: No interrupt multiplexing with other functions
description: GPIO interrupts are not multiplexed with other interrupt functions on this device. This ensures dedicated routing and reduces ambiguity when servicing events. Combined with bank enables, this simplifies interrupt configuration. It also improves determinism in interrupt latency. The absence of multiplexing is explicitly stated.
dependencies: Interrupt controller mapping (device-specific)
Remarks: Rely on direct mapping but still document vector assignments to avoid conflicts in system integration.

feature_name: Industry standards compliance (peripheral-level)
description: The GPIO peripheral itself is not compliant with any external connectivity standards. While software may implement standard protocols over GPIO, compliance is at the software layer. The hardware provides generic bit-level I/O primitives only. No protocol offload or standard timing is enforced by the module. This clarifies expectations about interoperability.
dependencies: None
Remarks: Use GPIO for custom or bit-banged protocols only; prefer dedicated peripherals for standards-compliant links.

feature_name: Synchronized edge detection and event timing
description: Edge detection for interrupts and EDMA events is synchronized to the GPIO peripheral clock. This provides deterministic sampling relative to the module clock domain. It avoids metastability but introduces a clock-period granularity in detection. Software should consider this when characterizing event latency. No additional hardware debouncing is provided.
evidence: Section 2.10.3 (edge detection synchronized to module clock)
dependencies: PSC clock frequency (CPU/6); ISR/EDMA setup
Remarks: Account for synchronization latency in time-critical designs; add external or software debouncing if required.

feature_name: Concurrency-safe multi-process output control
description: Separate set and clear data registers allow independent tasks to assert or deassert outputs without interfering with each other. Writes of 1 affect only targeted bits, avoiding read-modify-write races. This design reduces the need for critical sections in cooperative software. It enables atomic multi-bit updates across pins. Direct OUT_DATA writes remain available when exclusive access is guaranteed.
dependencies: Task scheduling discipline; DIR01 configured for outputs
Remarks: Adopt a coding guideline to prefer SET/CLR in shared contexts; reserve OUT_DATA for owned ports.

feature_name: Software-observable identification via PID
description: A read-only Peripheral Identification Register exposes function, scheme, and revision fields. This allows software to confirm the peripheral type and revision at runtime. The GPIO function code is 0x483 and revision is given by MAJOR.MINOR fields. Such identification aids in compatibility checks and conditional workarounds. The PID layout is fixed and read-only.
dependencies: None
Remarks: Read PID during driver init to log version and enable any revision-specific handling if necessary.
